the composition of them to be assigned to earnest men, or the opposite t were certainly ridiculous
for one to disapprove of the writing of earnest men, and approve of those, who are not such, engaging
in the work of composition. heopompus and imæus, who composed fables and slanders, and
picurus the leader of atheism, and ippona and rchilochus, are to be allowed to write in their
own shameful manner. ut he who proclaims the truth is to be prevented from leaving behind him
what is to benefit posterity. t is a good thing,  reckon, to leave to posterity good children. his is
the case with children of our bodies. ut words are the progeny of the soul. ence we call those
who have instructed us, fathers. isdom is a communicative and philanthropic thing. ccordingly,
olomon says, “y son, if thou receive the saying of my commandment, and hide it with thee,
thine ear shall hear wisdom.”   e points out that the word that is sown is hidden in the soul of
the learner, as in the earth, and this is spiritual planting. herefore also he adds, “nd thou shalt
apply thine heart to understanding, and apply it for the admonition of thy son.” or soul, methinks,
joined with soul, and spirit with spirit, in the sowing of the word, will make that which is sown
grow and germinate. nd every one who is instructed, is in respect of subjection the son of his
instructor. “on,” says he, “forget not my laws.” 
nd if knowledge belong not to all (set an ass to the lyre, as the proverb goes), yet written
compositions are for the many. “wine, for instance, delight in dirt more than in clean water.”
“herefore,” says the ord, “ speak to them in parables because seeing, they see not and hearing,
they hear not, and do not understand”   not as if the ord caused the ignorance for it were impious
to think so. ut e prophetically eposed this ignorance, that eisted in them, and intimated that
they would not understand the things spoken. nd now the aviour shows imself, out of is
abundance, dispensing goods to is servants according to the ability of the recipient, that they may
augment them by eercising activity, and then returning to reckon with them when, approving of
those that had increased is money, those faithful in little, and commanding them to have the charge
over many things, e bade them enter into the joy of the ord. ut to him who had hid the money,
entrusted to him to be given out at interest, and had given it back as he had received it, without
increase, e said, “hou wicked and slothful servant, thou oughtest to have given my money to
the bankers, and at my coming  should have received mine own.” herefore the useless servant
“shall be cast into outer darkness.”   “hou, therefore, be strong,” says aul, “in the grace that is
in hrist esus. nd the things which thou hast heard of me among many witnesses, the same
commit thou to faithful men, who shall be able to teach others also.”   nd again “tudy to show
thyself approved unto od, a workman that needeth not to be ashamed, rightly dividing the word
of truth.”

f, then, both proclaim the ord—the one by writing, the other by speech—are not both then
to be approved, making, as they do, faith active by love t is by one’s own fault that he does not
choose what is best od is free of blame. s to the point in hand, it is the business of some to lay
out the word at interest, and of others to test it, and either choose it or not. nd the judgment is
determined within themselves. ut there is that species of knowledge which is characteristic of the
herald, and that which is, as it were, characteristic of a messenger, and it is serviceable in whatever
way it operates, both by the hand and tongue. “or he that soweth to the pirit, shall of the pirit
reap life everlasting. nd let us not be weary in well-doing.”   n him who by ivine rovidence
meets in with it, it confers the very highest advantages,—the beginning of faith, readiness for
adopting a right mode of life, the impulse towards the truth, a movement of inquiry, a trace of
knowledge in a word, it gives the means of salvation. nd those who have been rightly reared in
the words of truth, and received provision for eternal life, wing their way to heaven. ost admirably,
therefore, the apostle says, “n everything approving ourselves as the servants of od as poor, and
yet making many rich as having nothing, yet possessing all things. ur mouth is opened to you.” 
“ charge thee,” he says, writing to imothy, “before od, and hrist esus, and the elect angels,
that thou observe these things, without preferring one before another, doing nothing by partiality.” 
oth must therefore test themselves the one, if he is qualified to speak and leave behind him
written records the other, if he is in a right state to hear and read as also some in the dispensation
of the ucharist, according to   custom enjoin that each one of the people individually should take
his part. ne’s own conscience is best for choosing accurately or shunning. nd its firm foundation
is a right life, with suitable instruction. ut the imitation of those who have already been proved,
and who have led correct lives, is most ecellent for the understanding and practice of the
commandments. “o that whosoever shall eat the bread and drink the cup of the ord unworthily,
shall be guilty of the body and blood of the ord. ut let a man eamine himself, and so let him
eat of the bread and drink of the cup.”   t therefore follows, that every one of those who undertake
to promote the good of their neighbours, ought to consider whether he has betaken himself to
teaching rashly and out of rivalry to any if his communication of the word is out of vainglory if
the the only reward he reaps is the salvation of those who hear, and if he speaks not in order to win
favour if so, he who speaks by writings escapes the reproach of mercenary motives. “or neither
at any time used we flattering words, as ye know,” says the apostle, “nor a cloak of covetousness.
od is witness. or of men sought we glory, neither of you, nor yet of others, when we might have

been burdensome as the apostles of hrist. ut we were gentle among you, even as a nurse cherisheth
her children.” 
n the same way, therefore, those who take part in the divine words, ought to guard against
betaking themselves to this, as they would to the building of cities, to eamine them out of curiosity
that they do not come to the task for the sake of receiving worldly things, having ascertained that
they who are consecrated to hrist are given to communicate the necessaries of life. ut let such
be dismissed as hypocrites. ut if any one wishes not to seem, but to be righteous, to him it belongs
to know the things which are best. f, then, “the harvest is plenteous, but the labourers few,” it is
incumbent on us “to pray” that there may be as great abundance of labourers as possible. 
ut the husbandry is twofold,—the one unwritten, and the other written. nd in whatever way
the ord’s labourer sow the good wheat, and grow and reap the ears, he shall appear a truly divine
husbandman. “abour,” says the ord, “not for the meat which perisheth, but for that which endureth
to everlasting life.”   nd nutriment is received both by bread and by words. nd truly “blessed
are the peace-makers,”   who instructing those who are at war in their life and errors here, lead
them back to the peace which is in the ord, and nourish for the life which is according to od,
by the distribution of the bread, those “that hunger after righteousness.” or each soul has its own
proper nutriment some growing by knowledge and science, and others feeding on the ellenic
philosophy, the whole of which, like nuts, is not eatable. “nd he that planteth and he that watereth,”
“being ministers” of im “that gives the increase, are one” in the ministry. “ut every one shall
receive his own reward, according to his own work. or we are od’s husbandmen, od’s husbandry.
e are od’s building,”   according to the apostle. herefore the hearers are not permitted to
apply the test of comparison. or is the word, given for investigation, to be committed to those
who have been reared in the arts of all kinds of words, and in the power of inflated attempts at
 
 proof whose minds are already pre-occupied, and have not been previously emptied. ut whoever
chooses to banquet on faith, is stedfast for the reception of the divine words, having acquired already
faith as a power of judging, according to reason. ence ensues to him persuasion in abundance.
nd this was the meaning of that saying of prophecy, “f ye believe not, neither shall ye
understand.”   “s, then, we have opportunity, let us do good to all, especially to the household
of faith.”   nd let each of these, according to the blessed avid, sing, giving thanks. “hou shalt
sprinkle me with hyssop, and  shall be cleansed. hou shalt wash me, and  shall be whiter than

shall rejoice. urn hy face from my sins. lot out mine iniquities. reate in me a clean heart, 
od, and renew a right spirit in my inward parts. ast me not away from hy face, and take not

e who addresses those who are present before him, both tests them by time, and judges by his
judgment, and from the others distinguishes him who can hear watching the words, the manners,
the habits, the life, the motions, the attitudes, the look, the voice the road, the rock, the beaten path,
the fruitful land, the wooded region, the fertile and fair and cultivated spot, that is able to multiply
the seed. ut he that speaks through books, consecrates himself before od, crying in writing thus
ot for gain, not for vainglory, not to be vanquished by partiality, nor enslaved by fear nor elated
by pleasure but only to reap the salvation of those who read, which he does, not at present participate
in, but awaiting in epectation the recompense which will certainly be rendered by im, who has
promised to bestow on the labourers the reward that is meet. ut he who is enrolled in the number
of men   ought not to desire recompense. or he that vaunts his good services, receives glory as
his reward. nd he who does any duty for the sake of recompense, is he not held fast in the custom
of the world, either as one who has done well, hastening to receive a reward, or as an evil-doer
avoiding retribution e must, as far as we can, imitate the ord. nd he will do so, who complies
with the will of od, receiving freely, giving freely, and receiving as a worthy reward the citizenship
itself. “he hire of an harlot shall not come into the sanctuary,” it is said accordingly it was forbidden
to bring to the altar the price of a dog. nd in whomsoever the eye of the soul has been blinded by
ill-nurture and teaching, let him advance to the true light, to the truth, which shows by writing the
things that are unwritten. “e that thirst, go to the waters,”   says saias. nd “drink water from
thine own vessels,”   olomon ehorts. ccordingly in “he aws,” the philosopher who learned
from the ebrews, lato, commands husbandmen not to irrigate or take water from others, until
they have first dug down in their own ground to what is called the virgin soil, and found it dry. or
it is right to supply want, but it is not well to support laziness. or ythagoras said that, “although
it be agreeable to reason to take a share of a burden, it is not a duty to take it away.”
ow the cripture kindles the living spark of the soul, and directs the eye suitably for
contemplation perchance inserting something, as the husbandman when he ingrafts, but, according
to the opinion of the divine apostle, eciting what is in the soul. “or there are certainly among us
many weak and sickly, and many sleep. ut if we judge ourselves, we shall not be judged.”   ow
this work of mine in writing is not artfully constructed for display but my memoranda are stored

up against old age, as a remedy against forgetfulness, truly an image and outline of those vigorous
and animated discourses which  was privileged to hear, and of blessed and truly remarkable men.
f these the one, in reece, an onic   the other in agna ræcia the first of these from
œle-yria, the second from gypt, and others in the ast. he one was born in the land of ssyria,
and the other a ebrew in alestine.
hen  came upon the last   (he was the first in power), having tracked him out concealed in
gypt,  found rest. e, the true, the icilian bee, gathering the spoil of the flowers of the prophetic
and apostolic meadow, engendered in the souls of his hearers a deathless element of knowledge.
ell, they preserving the tradition of the blessed doctrine derived directly from the holy apostles,
eter, ames, ohn, and aul, the sons receiving it from the father (but few were like the fathers),
came by od’s will to us also to deposit those ancestral and apostolic seeds. nd well  know that
they will eult  do not mean delighted with this tribute, but solely on account of the preservation
of the truth, according as they delivered it. or such a sketch as this, will,  think, be agreeable to
 
 a soul desirous of preserving from escape the blessed tradition.   “n a man who loves wisdom
the father will be glad.”   ells, when pumped out, yield purer water and that of which no one
partakes, turns to putrefaction. se keeps steel brighter, but disuse produces rust in it. or, in a
word, eercise produces a healthy condition both in souls and bodies. “o one lighteth a candle,
and putteth it under a bushel, but upon a candlestick, that it may give light to those who are regarded
worthy of the feast.”   or what is the use of wisdom, if it makes not him who can hear it wise
or still the aviour saves, “and always works, as e sees the ather.”   or by teaching, one
learns more and in speaking, one is often a hearer along with his audience. or the teacher of him
who speaks and of him who hears is one—who waters both the mind and the word. hus the ord
did not hinder from doing good while keeping the abbath   but allowed us to communicate of
those divine mysteries, and of that holy light, to those who are able to receive them. e did not
certainly disclose to the many what did not belong to the many but to the few to whom e knew
that they belonged, who were capable of receiving and being moulded according to them. ut secret
things are entrusted to speech, not to writing, as is the case with od.

nd if one say that it is written, “here is nothing secret which shall not be revealed, nor hidden
which shall not be disclosed,”   let him also hear from us, that to him who hears secretly, even
what is secret shall be manifested. his is what was predicted by this oracle. nd to him who is
able secretly to observe what is delivered to him, that which is veiled shall be disclosed as truth
and what is hidden to the many, shall appear manifest to the few. or why do not all know the truth
why is not righteousness loved, if righteousness belongs to all ut the mysteries are delivered
mystically, that what is spoken may be in the mouth of the speaker rather not in his voice, but in
his understanding. “od gave to the hurch, some apostles, and some prophets, and some evangelists,
and some pastors and teachers, for the perfecting of the saints, for the work of the ministry, for the
edifying of the body of hrist.” 
he writing of these memoranda of mine,  well know, is weak when compared with that spirit,
full of grace, which  was privileged to hear.   ut it will be an image to recall the archetype to
him who was struck with the thyrsus. or “speak,” it is said, “to a wise man, and he will grow wiser
and to him that hath, and there shall be added to him.” nd we profess not to eplain secret things
sufficiently—far from it—but only to recall them to memory, whether we have forgot aught, or
whether for the purpose of not forgetting. any things,  well know, have escaped us, through
length of time, that have dropped away unwritten. hence, to aid the weakness of my memory,
and provide for myself a salutary help to my recollection in a systematic arrangement of chapters,
 necessarily make use of this form. here are then some things of which we have no recollection
for the power that was in the blessed men was great.   here are also some things which remained
unnoted long, which have now escaped and others which are effaced, having faded away in the
mind itself, since such a task is not easy to those not eperienced these  revive in my commentaries.
ome things  purposely omit, in the eercise of a wise selection, afraid to write what  guarded
against speaking not grudging—for that were wrong—but fearing for my readers, lest they should
stumble by taking them in a wrong sense and, as the proverb says, we should be found “reaching
a sword to a child.” or it is impossible that what has been written should not escape, although
remaining unpublished by me. ut being always revolved, using the one only voice, that of writing,
they answer nothing to him that makes inquiries beyond what is written for they require of necessity
the aid of some one, either of him who wrote, or of some one else who has walked in his footsteps.
ome things my treatise will hint on some it will linger some it will merely mention. t will try
to speak imperceptibly, to ehibit secretly, and to demonstrate silently. he dogmas taught by
remarkable sects will be adduced and to these will be opposed all that ought to be premised in
accordance with the profoundest contemplation of the knowledge, which, as we proceed to the

renowned and venerable canon of tradition, from the creation of the world,   will advance to our
view setting before us what according to natural contemplation necessarily has to be treated of
beforehand, and clearing off what stands in the way of this arrangement. o that we may have our
ears ready for the reception of the tradition of true knowledge the soil being previously cleared of
the thorns and of every weed by the husbandman, in order to the planting of the vine. or there is
a contest, and the prelude to the contest and there are some mysteries before other mysteries.
 
 ur book will not shrink from making use of what is best in philosophy and other preparatory
instruction. “or not only for the ebrews and those that are under the law,” according to the
apostle, “is it right to become a ew, but also a reek for the sake of the reeks, that we may gain
all.”   lso in the pistle to the olossians he writes, “dmonishing every man, and teaching
every man in all wisdom, that we may present every man perfect in hrist.”   he nicety of
speculation, too, suits the sketch presented in my commentaries. n this respect the resources of
learning are like a relish mied with the food of an athlete, who is not indulging in luury, but
entertains a noble desire for distinction.
y music we harmoniously rela the ecessive tension of gravity. nd as those who wish to
address the people, do so often by the herald, that what is said may be better heard so also in this
case. or we have the word, that was spoken to many, before the common tradition. herefore we
must set forth the opinions and utterances which cried individually to them, by which those who
hear shall more readily turn.
nd, in truth, to speak briefly mong many small pearls there is the one and in a great take
of fish there is the beauty-fish and by time and toil truth will gleam forth, if a good helper is at
hand. or most benefits are supplied, from od, through men. ll of us who make use of our eyes
see what is presented before them. ut some look at objects for one reason, others for another. or
instance, the cook and the shepherd do not survey the sheep similarly for the one eamines it if it
be fat the other watches to see if it be of good breed. et a man milk the sheep’s milk if he need
sustenance let him shear the wool if he need clothing. nd in this way let me produce the fruit of
the reek erudition. 
or  do not imagine that any composition can be so fortunate as that no one will speak against
it. ut that is to be regarded as in accordance with reason, which nobody speaks against, with
reason. nd that course of action and choice is to be approved, not which is faultless, but which
no one rationally finds fault with. or it does not follow, that if a man accomplishes anything not
purposely, he does it through force of circumstances. ut he will do it, managing it by wisdom
divinely given, and in accommodation to circumstances. or it is not he who has virtue that needs

the way to virtue, any more than he, that is strong, needs recovery. or, like farmers who irrigate
the land beforehand, so we also water with the liquid stream of reek learning what in it is earthy
so that it may receive the spiritual seed cast into it, and may be capable of easily nourishing it. he
 tromata   will contain the truth mied up in the dogmas of philosophy, or rather covered over and
hidden, as the edible part of the nut in the shell. or, in my opinion, it is fitting that the seeds of
truth be kept for the husbandmen of faith, and no others.  am not oblivious of what is babbled by
some, who in their ignorance are frightened at every noise, and say that we ought to occupy ourselves
with what is most necessary, and which contains the faith and that we should pass over what is
beyond and superfluous, which wears out and detains us to no purpose, in things which conduce
nothing to the great end. thers think that philosophy was introduced into life by an evil influence,
for the ruin of men, by an evil inventor. ut  shall show, throughout the whole of these   tromata ,
that evil has an evil nature, and can never turn out the producer of aught that is good indicating
that philosophy is in a sense a work of ivine rovidence. 
  .—          
   .
 n reference to these commentaries, which contain as the eigencies of the case demand, the
ellenic opinions,  say thus much to those who are fond of finding fault. irst, even if philosophy
were useless, if the demonstration of its uselessness does good, it is yet useful. hen those cannot
condemn the reeks, who have only a mere hearsay knowledge of their opinions, and have not
entered into a minute investigation in each department, in order to acquaintance with them. or the
refutation, which is based on eperience, is entirely trustworthy. or the knowledge of what is
condemned is found the most complete demonstration. any things, then, though not contributing
to the final result, equip the artist. nd otherwise erudition commends him, who sets forth the most
essential doctrines so as to produce persuasion in his hearers, engendering admiration in those who
are taught, and leads them to the truth. nd such persuasion is convincing, by which those that love
learning admit the truth so that philosophy does not ruin life by being the originator of false practices
and base deeds, although some have calumniated it, though it be the clear image of truth, a divine
gift to the reeks   nor does it drag us away from the faith, as if we were bewitched by some
delusive art, but rather, so to speak, by the use of an ampler circuit, obtains a common eercise
 
 demonstrative of the faith. urther, the jutaposition of doctrines, by comparison, saves the truth,
from which follows knowledge.

hilosophy came into eistence, not on its own account, but for the advantages reaped by us
from knowledge, we receiving a firm persuasion of true perception, through the knowledge of
things comprehended by the mind. or  do not mention that the   tromata , forming a body of varied
erudition, wish artfully to conceal the seeds of knowledge. s, then, he who is fond of hunting
captures the game after seeking, tracking, scenting, hunting it down with dogs so truth, when
sought and got with toil, appears a delicious   thing. hy, then, you will ask, did you think it fit
that such an arrangement should be adopted in your memoranda ecause there is great danger in
divulging the secret of the true philosophy to those, whose delight it is unsparingly to speak against
everything, not justly and who shout forth all kinds of names and words indecorously, deceiving
themselves and beguiling those who adhere to them. “or the ebrews seek signs,” as the apostle
says, “and the reeks seek after wisdom.” 
  .—  .
 here is a great crowd of this description some of them, enslaved to pleasures and willing to
disbelieve, laugh at the truth which is worthy of all reverence, making sport of its barbarousness.
ome others, ealting themselves, endeavour to discover calumnious objections to our words,
furnishing captious questions, hunters out of paltry sayings, practicers of miserable artifices,
wranglers, dealers in knotty points, as that bderite says—
“or mortals’ tongues are glib, and on them are many speeches
nd a wide range for words of all sorts in this place and that.”
nd—
“f whatever sort the word you have spoken, of the same sort you must hear.”
nflated with this art of theirs, the wretched ophists, babbling away in their own jargon toiling
their whole life about the division of names and the nature of the composition and conjunction of
sentences, show themselves greater chatterers than turtle-doves scratching and tickling, not in a
manly way, in my opinion, the ears of those who wish to be tickled.
“ river of silly words—not a dropping”
just as in old shoes, when all the rest is worn and is falling to pieces, and the tongue alone remains.
he thenian olon most ecellently enlarges, and writes—
“ook to the tongue, and to the words of the glozing man,
ut you look on no work that has been done
ut each one of you walks in the steps of a fo,

nd in all of you is an empty mind.”
his,  think, is signified by the utterance of the aviour, “he foes have holes, but the on of
man hath not where to lay is head.”   or on the believer alone, who is separated entirely from
the rest, who by the cripture are called wild beasts, rests the head of the universe, the kind and
gentle ord, “who taketh the wise in their own craftiness. or the  knoweth the thoughts of

nd as appears to me, it was in consequence of perceiving the great benefit which is conferred

all the rahmins, and the drysi, and the etæ. nd such were strictly deified by the race of the

kind of royalty, inferior to that administration which is purely rational and divine, which brings to

odium profitentur continentiam. rius autem tractandum est de prima parte. uod si quodlibet vitæ

vitæ genus sectari, manifestum est eam, quæ temperanter et secundum virtutem agitur, longe
tutissimam esse. am cum “domino sabbati,” etiamsi intemperanter vivat, nulla ratio reddenda sit,
multo magis qui vitam moderate et temperate instituit, nulli erit rationi reddendæ obnoius. “mnia
enim licent, sed non omnia epediunt,”   ait postolus. uod si omnia licent, videlicet moderatum
quoque esse et temperantem. uemadmodum ergo is est laudandus, qui libertate sua usus est ad
vivendum e virtute ita multo magis qui dedit nobis liberam nostri potestatem, et concessit vivere
ut vellemus, est venerandus et adorandus, quod non permiserit, ut nostra electio et vitatio cuiquam
necessario serviret. i est autem uterque æque securus, et qui incontinentiam, et qui continentiam
elegerit, non est tamen e æquo honestum et decorum. ui enim impegit in voluptates, gratificatur
corpori temperans autem animam corporis dominam liberat a perturbationibus. t si dicant nos
“vocatos fuisse in libertatem, solummodo ne præbeamus libertatem, in occasionem carni,”   e
sententia postoli. i autem cupiditati est obsequendum, et quæ probrosa estet turpis vita tanquam
indifferens est eligenda, ut ipsi dicunt aut cupiditatibus est omnino parendum, et si hoc ita est,
facienda sunt quævis impudicissima et maime nefaria, eos sequendo, qui nobis persuadent ant
sunt aliquæ declinandæ cupiclitates, et non est amplius vivendum indifferenter, neque est impudenter
serviendum vilissimis et abjectissimis nostris partibus, ventri et pudendis, dum cupidate ducti nostro
blandimur cadaveri. utritur enim et vivificatur cupiditas, dum ei voluptates ministrantur
quemadmodum rursus si impediatur et interturbetur, flaccescit. uomodo autem fieri potest, ut qui
victus est a voluptatibus corporis, omino assimiletur, ant ei habeat cognitionem mnis enim
voluptatis principium est cupiditas cupiditas autem est molestia et sollicitudo, quæ propter egestatem

curaque est gerenda animæ, qua apud solum eum perseverandum est. ens enim, quæ est munda
et ab omni vitio libera, est quodammodo apta ad potestatem ei suscipiendam, cum divina in ea
assurgat imago “t quicunque habet hanc spem in omino, seipsum,” inquit, “mundum castumque
facit, quatenus ille est castus.”   t ii autem accipiant ei cognitionem, qui adhuc ducuntur ab
affectibus, minime potest fieri ergo nec ut finem assequantur, cum nullam habeant ei cognitionem.
t eum quidem, qui hunc finem non assequitur, accusare videtur ei ignoratio ut eus autem
 
 ignoretur, efficit vitæ institutio. mnino enim fieri non potest, ut quis simul sit et scientia præditus,
et blandiri corpori non erubescat. eque enim potest unquam convenire, quod voluptas sit bonum,
cure eo, quod bonum sit solum pulchrum et honesturn vel etiam cure eo, quod solus sit pulcher
ominus, et solus bonus ens, et solus amabilis. “n hristo autem circumcisi estis, circumcisione
non manu facta, in espoliatione corporis carnis, in circumcisione hristi.   i ergo cum hristo
consurreistis, quæ sursum sunt quærite, quæ sursum sunt sapite, non quæ sunt super terram. ortui

“ortificate ergo membra, quæ sunt super terram, fornicationem, immunditiam, passionem,

 umana ergo continentia,   ea, inquam, quæ est e sententia philosophorum ræcorum,
profitetur pugnare cum cupiditate, et in factis ei non inservire quæ est autem e nostra sententia
continentia, non concupiscere non ut quis concupiscens se fortiter gerat, sed ut etiam a
concupiscendo se contineat. on potest autem ea aliter comparari continentia, nisi gratia ei. t
ideo diit “etite, et dabitur vobis.”   anc gratiam oyses quoque accepit, qui indigo corpore
erat indutus, ut quadraginta diebus neque esuriret, neque sitiret. uemadmodum autem melius est
sanum esse, quam ægrotantem disserere de sanitate ita lucem esse, quam loqui de luce et quæ est
e veritate continentia, ea quæ docetur a philosophis. on enim ubi est lu, illic tenebræ ubi autem
sola insidet cupiditas, etiamsi quiescat a corporea operatione, at memoria cure eo, quod non est
præsens, congreditur. eneratim autem nobis procedar oratio de matrimonio, nutrimento, et aliis,
ut nihil faciamus e cupiditate, velimus autem ea sola, quæ sunt necessaria. on sumus enim filii
cupiditatis, sed voluntatis et eum, qui uorem duit propter liberorum procreationem, eercere
oportet continentiam, ut ne suam quidem concupiscat uorem, quam debet diligere, honesta et
moderata voluntate operam dans liberis. on enim “carnis curam gerere ad concupiscentias”

cum quibus est, quæ omnia considerat, ei potestas, absque divisione cadens in divisionem. ui

 “benedicenti, et maledices maledicenti.” uando autem his, ira scilicet et cupidirate, superior factus,
et creaturæ amore vere affectus propter eum, qui est eus et effector omnium, gnostice vitam
instituerit, et alvatori similis evadens, facilem temperantiæ habitum acquisiverit, et cognitionem,
fidem, ac dilectionem conjunerit, simplici hac in parte judicio utens, et vere spiritalis factus, nec
earum quæ e ira et cupiditate procedunt, cogitationum omnino capa, ad omini imaginem ab
ipso artifice efficitur homo perfectus, is sane dignus jam est, qui frater a omino nominetur, is
simul est amicus et filius. ic ergo “duo et tres” in eodem “congregantur,” nempe in homine gnosrico.
oterit etiam multorum quoque concordia e tribus æstimata, cum quibus est ominus, significare
unam cclesiam, unum hominem, genus unum. nnon cum uno quidem udæo erat ominus, cum
legera tulit at prophetans, et eremiam mittens abylonem, quinetiam cos qui erant e gentibus
vocans per prophetiam, congregavit duos populos tertius autem est unus, qui e duobus “creatur
in riorum hominem, quo inambulat et inhabitat” in ipsa cclesia t le simul et prophetæ, una
cum vangelio, in nomine hristi congregantur in unam cognitionem. ui ergo propter odium
uorem non ducunt, vel propter concupiscentiam carne indifferenter abutuntur, non sunt in numero
illorum qui servantur, cum quibus est ominus.

quoque, sed non eo modo, quo vult ille, dissolvens legem ut alterius ei sed idem vir et ominus,
dum vetera renovat, non amplius concedit polygamiam (nam hanc quidem epetebat eus, quando
oportebat homines augeri et multiplicari), sed monogamiam introducit prompter liberorum
procreationem et domus curam, ad quam data est mulier adjutri et si cui postolus propter
intemperantiam et ustionem, veniam secundi concedit matrimonii nam hic quoque non peccat
quidem e estamento (non est enim a lege prohibitus), non implet autem summam illam vitæ
perfectionem, quæ agitur e vangelio. loriam autem sibi acquirit cœlestem, qui apud se manserit,
earn, quæ est morte dissoluta, impollutam servans conjunctionem, et grato ac lubente animo paret
ceconomiæ, per quam effectum est, ut divelli non possit a omini ministerio. ed nec eum, qui e
conjugali surgit cubili, similiter ut olim, tingi nunc quoque jubet divina per ominum providentia
non enim necessario a liberorum abducit procreatione, qui credentes per unum baptismum ad
consuetudinem omni e parte perfectam abluit, ominus, qui etiam multa oysis baptismata per
unum comprehendit baptismum. roinde le, ut per carnalem generationem nostram præ diceret
regenerationera, genitali seminis facultati baptismum olim adhibuit, non vero quod ab hominis
generatione abhorreret. uod enim apparet homo generatus, hoc valet seminis dejectio. on sunt
ergo multi coitus genitales, sed matricis susceptio fatetur generationem, cum in naturæ officina
semen formatur in fetum. uomodo autem vetus quidera est solum matrimonium et legis inventum,
alienum autem est, quod est e omino, matrimonium, cum idem eus servetur a nobis “on”
